video_title: Peter Boockvar: The AI Spending Bubble Is Cracking
transcript: the the bubble is clearly in the capex side. You can be sure in the next couple years this DEM cycle will reverse. I I think debts and deficits now matter. Markets are choking on excess supply for the first time in my lifetime. So I think gold's a buy on this pullback. It's the rise in rates, real rates that have been sort of the the the biggest sort of bugaboo for for gold here. Hi everyone, I'm Maggie Lake. Joining me to discuss global markets is Peter Bookvar, chief investment officer at OnePoint BFG Wealth Partners. Hi Peter, it's great to see you again. >> Hey Maggie, great to be with you again. >> A warm welcome to everyone tuning in. If you are not a member of our community, click the link to keep up with the latest and get access to exclusive content and invites. Uh so Peter, a lot going on. It feels like it's not so calm and quiet this summer. It feels like there's kind of a lot of turbulence as you look AC across global markets. What's top of your radar? >> Well, I I think in investing is getting harder. Uh and and I say that, you know, not like hitting around, but it is in the sense that you usually markets give you sort of like these one decision situations. You know, Mac 7 was a one decision situation. Now, it was great if you own them. It wasn't so great if you didn't own them. uh then then all of a sudden markets sort of differentiated and diversified away from mag 7 and then opened up a whole world of of opportunities but and but then post war with the dollar rallying uh it made international investing more difficult and then everyone piled into semiconductor stocks and you were either you know at the party owning them or you were left outside uh which is this very interesting dynamic where you know max 7 continues to weak and let's own Micron for example. So, uh it's this this this strange world that we're in. I think we're all trying to figure out at this point is I feel like we're reaching some major inflection points with this AI trade. >> Uh and I do notice that investors are beginning to uh look for some defensive areas of the market too and uh even that chip trade is beginning to falter it seems or get tired and so uh really interesting dynamics here I have to say. >> Yeah. And I think that's why, you know, we're having a lot of these conversations trying to help people figure it out because if if you've done well, you want to hold on to that, but the future looks really uncertain and some of the things you would do to diversify, it it's not clear whether that's working. So, let's start with what you see in the AI trade. Um, are you like everyone else worried about a bubble? Um, do you have exposure? Have you reduced exposure? recommending reducing exposure. What kind of, you know, riskreward profile do you see in that space? >> Well, the bubble is clearly in the capex side, not necessarily the technology because at the end of the day, we're all going to benefit from the technology. Just a question of when I say we, meaning I believe the users of it. >> I think the spenders on this, that's where the bubble is. And taking a step back, I I think bells started to ring on the mag 7 trade last year on the hyperscaler trade because I I need to include Oracle in this. last year, late last year, when people realized that that half of Oracle's massive RPO, which is the remaining performance obligations, which initially when they they talked about it in a couple of quarters prior that sent the stock north of 300, people realized like, wow, you know, half of your exposure is open AI. Mhm. >> Not only that, you're spending half your revenue on capex, which then went to 75% of your revenue on capex and now is 100% of your revenue on capex. A little less than that if you include some prepayments they're bringing in. Then a couple quarters last year, Meta reported stellar numbers, but the stock got hammered because of worries about all their capex spend. And then fast forward into this year and markets certainly started to differentiate between the 700 plus billion of spend and let's punish those that are spending it and let's reward the recipients of that spend mostly semiconductor stocks that originally was Nvidia and Broadcom and then shift it to memory and storage names and then also the Dells and the HPs that are selling a lot of the data center servers and and other equipment into these data centers. So that that's how I think investors are sort of hopping around. But now we're at a situation where Micron has 85% gross margins and investors are saying, well, how sustainable is an 85% gross margin in what is historically been a highly cyclical space? And yeah, the supply is coming. The bulls will say, "But it's not coming until 2027, 2028. Therefore, we're okay right now." And the bulls will say, "Well, it's trading at seven times earnings micron, and you still got to buy it." And I say, "Well, okay. Um, never tell me what a PE ratio is on a cyclical stock and tell me it's cheap." Because the way that you are supposed to invest in a cyclical industry is you're supposed to buy a stock when the PE is really high because that implies a cyclical company is under earning probably in a down cycle. And when the PE is very low, it means that they're probably over earning with excessively high profit margins because that's the top of a cycle. That's how investors should be looking at this. And you should also be pricing a stock in a highly cyclical sector relative to book value and sales. And Micron's trading at about 10 times sales or or I should say at its peak was trading 10 times sales. And for those that weren't around in 1999 2000, it peaked at about nine to 10 times >> sales. Uh so and I think I need to to to say too that anybody investing in uh DRAM and in NA and NAN storage like Western Dig or or Seagate um make sure you educate yourself on the Chinese companies that are in the same space because they're coming for your gross margin. This is this is I think uh you and I spoke briefly something that we don't hear enough about but it's been coming up in conversations that I'm having that you know this this sort of model of market dominance and being willing to uh sacrifice on price or underpric competitors. We know that ch has been so powerful for China. Um, are they at the point where they pose or what kind of competitive threat do you think Chinese companies in the AI space pose to the current market leaders? >> Well, on a software uh LLM model perspective, they are clearly taking a lot of market share uh because they've been able to create these models through distillation at a fraction of the price that we're spending here. So is that like a deep seek when you say that >> the deep seek the miniax the the the Quinn from Alibaba uh the the um uh Shipu who's got the uh the DM is it the DM model? Yes. So uh there are multitude of models that are being offered at a fraction of the price. A fraction being defined as 75 to 80% cheaper than US models. So that's the software side. On the hardware side, well, it's clear that in areas outside of tech like EVs and solar panels and automation and robotics um and electrical equipment and so forth, on the hardware side, China's competing with everybody. It's not just that. You you if you if you're a farmer in Brazil, you don't need to you don't no longer need to buy a deer tractor. You can call up China and get one of their tractors at a fraction of the price. And on the memory and the storage side, uh, you know, a few days after SKHENX uh, had their US listing and they're already public in South Korea, but they're US listing and everyone's talking about SKH Heinix. No one's talking about the IPO, I think on Thursday of the week that we're taping this of CXMT uh, in Hong Kong, which is the fourth largest global DRAM producer. And investors need to understand that that that a lot of times in a lot of different businesses, China's first focus is market share. It's not profit margin, >> right? >> And as Jeff Bezos once said, your profit margin is my opportunity. And when Micron's deliver delivering an 85% gross margin, you know, that's what I was referring to before is that gross margin is my market share. And that's what CXMT and soon to be followed by Yangy memory technology which is more focused on the NAND storage side business that's coming after SanDisk and Western Ditch. Now, this may take time for them to to to gain market share, but I think that investors in the US companies should really familiarize themselves with these Chinese companies because again, these are still very highly cyclical businesses and China is going to focus first on market share and profitability secondarily. >> It's so important. Do I are there national security concerns that would slow adoption or do you think while that might be an issue here in the US and they might be blocked from this market the rest of the world is perfectly happy to jump on a cheaper alternative for AI or AI hardware >> right well I think at least on the software model side because it's open source everyone can see what's in it >> so from that perspective there's less national security worries. But you make the right point and something that I've been arguing as well is that this is not just China, you know, and the US and and competing model to model and are we going to use US models here only or integrate some lowerc cost Chinese models as well which is happening. It's the competition for the global customer. It's trying to get customers both on the hardware side and the software side in Europe, in Latin America, in Asia. Because when you think about being able to provide a a good enough model at a fraction of the price, good enough could be just fine. Particularly for companies that are smaller, that have more limited budgets. They're going to go with the cheapest alternative, assuming that that alternative is still very good. And I think China is proving that what they're bringing to the tech table uh is still very good. >> Do you think US investors understand or are paying enough attention to that competitive threat? Well, on the day we're taping this and everyone's still talking about SK, uh, I have not heard on any of of of the media anybody mentioned CXMT, which is shocking to me that they're talking about semis generally, but no mention of this upcoming IPO in a few days. So, I think some are paying attention, uh, those that that have a global lens. Uh, but I think that there are others that are not. This is why we love talking to you, Peter, and I read your substack religiously because you do always keep your eye on global market and and you have to in the in the world we're living in. It's it's crazy not to. How how might this impact geopolitics? I had a I had a European say something very interesting to me recently and very controversial which it will probably sort of not be comfortable for folks in the US to hear but that when it comes to tr who you trust with your data they weren't so sure that they that they trusted the US more than the than the Chinese given the state of geopolitics right now perhaps not surprising given the Trump administration's sort of antagonistic uneasy relationship with Europe but does this th the threat threaten to put the the US and China on a collision course when it comes to sort of economic warfare if if this if the Chinese AI companies pose that much of a competitive threat. >> Well, it's interesting because that you know the the word trust can mean a few different things here. Uh it's it's when it comes to your privacy, I would not be trusting the Chinese. On the other hand, when it comes to trade, a lot of foreigners saying, "I can't trust the US >> because look what look what they've done to me." Me being a foreign country or a foreign company, look what they've done to me on the tariff side. Mhm. >> So, I'm going to wake up one day and and and and uh I'm going to get slapped with a tariff and I'm not going to be able to source a particular input into my business from uh an American company because now I got to pay 25% more through a tariff. So, there there multitude multiple trust issues that I think businesses have with with both sides. M >> but I think importantly though whether one agrees with the tariffs or not what we did to foreign countries and companies was force them to diversify their supply chains. >> Yep. Because whether again whether you agree with our purpose of using them or not, if you're a German company, if you're a Singaporean company, if you're a Brazilian company and you're getting slapped with tariffs just because you woke up in the morning, well, you are going to have other alternatives to US suppliers because that's just the natural thing to do because you're at the end of the day trying to protect your business. You're trying to protect your profit margins. You're trying to get maintain your access to the lowest cost of goods sold. It's a great point and I know it's a point you've been making on tariffs all along. You know, there are always um you know consequences that go beyond the initial policy aim I think that people are still trying to sort of you know game out. Would you uh be a buyer of those IPOs when they come to market in Hong Kong or are you just tracking it based on the impact they may have on the landscape? >> So from an an investor standpoint um the DRAM business is just too fast for me. I am just not cool enough and I don't have the stomach to because to me this is like the height of cyclicality and uh I I I leave that to others. So I'm going to be watching it from a signaling standpoint. I'm going to be because we know that semis in the US now make up about 18 to n 19% of the S&P 500. So from an index perspective, you have no choice but to follow this, you know, as opposed to other areas of semis where they're more sort of specialty rather than commodity based uh where Micron is such a commodity- based provider. Now Micron and the bulls will tell you well you know they're locking up these these contracts uh in terms of volume and price. Well, volume, yes. Pricing wise though, they're giving like a ceiling and a floor. And Micro will tell you that while they're locking up these contracts, that 40% is locked up, which means 60% of the revenue is still subject to the vagaries of pricing. Now I think people need to understand that with when it comes to memory if you look at the demand for memory on a on a volume basis not price but volume since the mid90s when the PC demand really took off and Micron and semis had their previous epic bull market the the demand for memory is is pretty linear and now maybe it's taking another leg up because of the data center demand but it always goes up the demand for compute has been going up forever. ever. But because there's always these supply demand imbalances, the pricing of DRAM typically goes down. I mean, you look at a TV, right? As the world's middle class grows, as more people get access to electricity, they want a TV. Now, maybe some of them will use their iPad or whatever, but generally speaking, the demand for TVs goes up. But we know the pricing always goes down. But that's the history of technology. Technology is this incredible deflationary force and it has been forever. So the demand for memory will continue to go up at infinitum but the pricing typically goes down and you can be sure in the next couple years. This DEM cycle will reverse the volume demand will continue up but the pricing will go down and you will see a crushing of Micron's earnings. I'm picking a micron as opposed to others, but I'm just using them basically as an example here of being in the business of a highly commoditized product even with these contracts that they're signing. >> Yeah, that's exactly what I was thinking about. It is it is a commodity market. Uh so if if you're not cool enough for that, uh are you watching financials? How do you feel about financials? Because we have banks are going to start reporting earnings here in the US. How do you feel about that sector? So banks are interesting because banks have been rallying around the world. Uh banks of Japan continue to rally because you have that very steep yield curve where the the BOJ continues to drag their feet when it comes to raising short-term rates only to 1% and long-term yields continue to go up. So they're benefiting there. Then you have banks in Europe which are hitting 52- week highs because now the ECB is raising rates and and and allowing them to to get some some interest income and also to have a pretty decent spread. Negative interest rates were a killer for European banks and now having them further away from negative is good. Canadian banks are having their day. Uh and US banks are now rallying too, but US banks are particularly getting help from robust capital markets, particularly with the with the big companies. and and and now we're getting we had that that that um yield spread that narrowed dramatically when Kevin Worsh had his first meeting, but now we're widening out again, which could be a help for banks. So, and also the the the the investor desire to now begin to diversify. You know, they don't want to be in hyperscalers because of deteriorating cash flows. They're getting nervous being overindexed to semis which have had such a obviously an extraordinary move as we know. and they're looking for other things because it's not just banks that are now rallying. Consumer staple stocks are rallying now. Healthc care stocks are now beginning to rally. So the there there more choices I think for for investors here that I think uh are becoming interesting. >> Yeah. Do do you um do you feel as strongly about any of the sectors or do you have to be a stock picker here within those sectors? >> So I've been loving the consumer staple stocks. um they've been thrown out the window and to define that uh Craft Hind which is one of our our holdings which has just been a disastrous stock over the past many years ever since 3G merged the two companies together and all you have to do is ask Warren Buffett too about that investment. Um but now you have 22 analysts that cover it. I might have said this to you before and only one has it as a buy. But you have a relatively new CEO that's investing in the business that has a great uh dividend yield that is well covered in terms of its dividend payout ratio. Uh and you have a lot of uh stocks that are that have been punished. You know, for good reason. They've been punished. Volumes have been tough. The consumer, they overindex lower income consumers and they've been challenged. So there are reasons why these stocks have gotten hit. But now I think that they've been thrown out to the point where they're dirt cheap with generous uh dividend yields and I do think a stabilization in their businesses that I think are be will be reflected when we see uh earnings coming uh over the next couple weeks. You take Pepsi for example, stock fell earnings, but Pepsi really highlighted, yes, the US business is still challenged by the consumer, but they're maneuvering around that and their international business is doing great. And I think you're going to find that with a lot of these these global food companies is that international is overindexing relative to the US, but it's still going to be growth at the end of the day. >> So interesting. What do you what do you how do you feel about the economic backdrop and inflation backdrop uh in terms of what it'll do for earnings. >> So when we talk about earnings growth, of course, you know, we we talk in nominal terms. So inflation has a benefit to that. Uh, speaking of earnings and and I I'll get to answering question in a second. When we look at earnings in Q1, 28% astonishing growth and it gets reported 28% growth. >> But it's important to look what drove that 12 percentage points of the 28% was other income with these big hyperscalers. other income being the mark-to-market gains that these companies have in their ownership of SpaceX, Anthropic, and Open AI. >> So 28% take out 12, you get 16%. >> I think about half of that, give or take, was semiconductor growth, earnings growth. So you take out that and you're talking about mid to high singledigit earnings growth. That's how people should be looking at Q2 because you have the same influences of other income and semiconductor driven growth. Now and and then we know that middle that mid to high singledigit nominal earnings growth some of that of course is inflation. M >> now inflation is interesting here because particularly with the commodity trade with the war is you have obviously this very sharp pullback in oil prices but product prices that remaining very high and yes they've come in off their highs but you know the average gallon of gasoline is still 380ish >> you know it's still up 25% plus from where it was pre-war where the you know the average gallon of I'm sorry the average barrel of oil is only up about 10 to 15% off where it was mid60 level uh pre-war but but jet fuel and diesel prices are up even more so the inflation picture is still very complicated I believe for the Fed >> and why I think they're certainly not cutting but I disagree with the market and thinking that they're going to hike I think Kevin Worsh is just going to sit tight for a while >> until he sees until he gets a sense because he's kind of in a hard spot right if he hikes, he'll have the fury of the Trump administration. Um, but if he sounds too doubbish, he's going to lose credibility. >> And also, I don't think he did this on purpose. And I and and I agree with these task forces he's created, but how do you make a monetary move before you hear from the task forces? If you have a task force on measuring inflation and economic data, well, how can you change policy before the task force tells you what they think? and these task forces may go through year end. So that gives me another reason why I think they're doing nothing this year. >> It's a great point. It's a great point that not a lot of people have brought up. He's almost sort of, you know, tied his hands purposely until he and he can and he can use that, right? He can say, I want to hear from from these experts outside the Fed as well about what's going on before we make a move. Do you think the bond market will will take their lead or could we see upward pressure on yields if things are running hot and the Fed's not moving? >> Well, what's so interesting right now about the US Treasury market is inflation expectations in the TIPS market is very muted. At the same time, we seemingly go up every single day in the US 10 year, which means that real rates are what's really going up. And I can't argue that real rates are going up because the economy is robust. Because outside of the data center build and upper income spend, the economy is two-ish% growth. So this rise in real rates is rather fascinating. And I think it's because that's in deficits matter now. And you see that in Japan and you see that in France, you see that in the UK, you see that in Germany in terms of their bond yields. So that's what's really interesting is so I want to say that the long end is tightening for the Fed, but they're not tightening because the labor market's robust. We saw that in a pretty mediocre labor market a few weeks ago. Well, is it tightening because of inflation? Well, the TIPS market's not reflecting that. So, this rise in long-term interest rates to me is really noteworthy and I can't necessarily connect the dot to new found worries about inflation. I think inflation worries are still there. I think they're sticky and persistent, but they haven't accelerated. >> Is it a loss of confidence? >> I I think debts and deficits now matter. Maybe it's the, you know, the flood of hyperscaler debt issuance that's leading to uh maybe hedging in the treasury market. Maybe I I I maybe it's okay, you know, we we want to spend an extra half a trillion dollars on defense. You know, you you you look at maybe it's Germany that is now, you know, further accelerating, you know, their defense spending. >> Uh Takichi in Japan talks about, you know, more spending there. The the new the new prime minister, Andy Burnham, talks about maybe an expanded budget. So I think maybe markets are choking on excess supply for the first time in my lifetime. >> Wow, that's a big statement. Why isn't gold doing better if that's the case? >> Well, gold is doesn't do well in a rising real rate environment. So I think that that's the the the the sort of the the issue that gold is facing is rising real rates and you know the dollar has bounced. Now with the dollar dollar really hasn't bounced much. You know I I think the dollar is a sell. If this is if we entered the year with the Fed funds futures market pricing in two rate cuts now we are pricing in about one and a half rate increases and the dollar went from 97 to 101. That's not really much of a rally. >> So I do think eventually this real rate move will end and then inflation will catch up to nominal rates. And I do think that this modest dollar rally is going to roll over again. So I think gold's a buy on this pullback. Uh I expected a pullback because anything that goes up in a straight line is due for a rest. And we know gold and silver went up in straight lines uh in late last year and and early this year. So there was a there there was a a rest period coming and we're certainly seeing it. Um, but I do think that it's the rise in rates, real rates that have been sort of the the the biggest sort of bugaboo for for gold here. >> Yeah. You uh you t you you've been writing a bit about Japan. You just mentioned Japan and what's been happening there and you mentioned a little earlier with JGBs and I just wanted to circle back to that. Um you you rightly point out that you know this this threat of Japanese repatriation has kind of been dangling out there and there was a report that once again kind of got walked back but um is this something that's a risk? Do you see the what do you what are you looking at when you look at the Japanese market because it's so important but how do what are you keeping an eye on? What do you think the implications are? >> Well there there are a couple things let's take the yen which is now 162 so is at 40year lows against the dollar. We have the Bank of Japan that is just continuing to slow crawl the rise in short-term interest rates relative to inflation. So that is further weakening the yen which then in turn is further inflaming inflation and we're seeing the long end the rest of the yield curve tighten for the BOJ. So, we're heading to a point where the Japanese investor, whether it's the institution, the pension fund like the GPIF, which is the largest pension fund in the world with $1.8 trillion of assets, are going to say, "Hey, well, Japanese GGBs are much more uh attractive than owning US treasuries or French oats, for example, and I'm going to take that money home." Japan is the third largest net creditor nation in the world with a net investment position, positive investment position of $3.5 trillion. And then you have the news where it was a comment last week from the Japanese finance minister that said, "Hey, Japan and Japanese investors and pension funds, why don't you bring that money home? You have so much money invested overseas relative to foreign investment in Japan. Bring some home. We have a great stock market. We have a cheap currency and we have these now attractive JGB yields. Then that was followed up on on on Monday, the day we're taping this. And then they said, "Okay, maybe not so fast." And and we're not going to force you to do it. It'll be your choice, but just think about it. M >> so I I think we're getting stretched here on JGB yields that at some point money is going to come back. I think the yen is just way way too cheap and the BOJ is going to have to do something and and maybe continuously raise rates this year which will eventually bring that money back. And this has reverberations for global bond markets too because Japan was the home of interest rate repression for many many years that Ben Bernani and Mario Draghi that thought were such a good idea I say sarcastically that they wanted to repeat it uh uh in the US and Europe. Um but these are all things that that that investors need to focus on. Even if you're not directly invested in Japan, uh if you're invested in anything fixed income, uh and you're invested in stocks around the world, Japan owns a lot of foreign stocks outside of Japan. And if that money comes home, then then then we have some major flow of fund issues that we need to pay attention to. >> Yeah. Is it can they can they even make a small change without it destabilizing the the global financial center? because we've seen little little uh episodes of a potential to unwind what they call the carry trade, right? And and it's just wre havoc. I mean, you know, a couple summers ago, we saw a big uh a lot of unrest when it appeared that that was going to happen very can can it can Japan make that kind of pivot without it causing a crisis? So, a couple of years ago, we did have sort of the carry trade blow up. And ever since then, I never can get a real sense of how big that trade is today. What's left of it, even though you'd think that maybe people have re-uped it with the the the the subsequent yen weakness again, I'm not sure. And with respect to the asset allocation, I think it would happen on the margin, but that's where prices set on the margin. So you take the GPIF the government pension investment fund there loosely they have an allocation between bonds and equities 25% domestic equities 25% foreign equities 25% domestic JGBs and and other you know corporate bonds there and 25% foreign bonds now there's room at least on the fixed income side to them to move that around they could still within remain within some parameters increase their domestic stake bond purchases or just buy less and that could have capital flow implications here. So I do think that we're getting to a point where particularly with that Japanese finance minister news is that the dollar weakness is probably at least versus the yen is probably running its course and we have to see what sort of a reversal that may lead to at some point not just in the yen dollar trade but also what this means for global interest rates. Yeah, it's a it's it's something we're going to have to keep a close eye on. Um, Peter, we always appreciate catching up with you because you're watching stuff and digging into the details in a way that no one else is. So, thank you so much for taking the time today. We appreciate it. >> Thanks, Maggie. Always great chatting with you.
